sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Preterm labour refers to labour before 37 weeks gestation. The major prehospital issues are recognising when birth is truly imminent, expediting obstetric transfer when it is not, and preparing for a premature neonate when birth cannot be avoided.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Regular contractions or escalating pelvic pressure before 37 weeks gestation
- Vaginal fluid loss, bleeding, mucus plug loss, or rupture of membranes
- Lower back pain or abdominal pain
- Visible presenting part or urge to push if birth is imminent

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Not all preterm contractions lead to birth, so transport may still be the main priority
- The earlier the gestation, the greater the neonatal risk from respiratory failure, hypothermia, and the need for specialist care
- If birth appears unavoidable, preparation for neonatal resuscitation and warmth becomes critical
- Malpresentation, cord prolapse, and postpartum haemorrhage may complicate preterm delivery

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient appropriately and minimise aortocaval compression in later pregnancy
- Perform frequent reassessment and document contraction pattern and progression if practical
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- If birth is not imminent, prioritise prompt obstetric-capable transport
- If birth is imminent, prepare for childbirth, neonatal warmth, and newborn resuscitation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG where clinically useful for maternal monitoring
- Gain IV access if the patient is unstable, delivery is imminent, or complications are suspected
- Consider analgesia and anti-emetic therapy within scope where appropriate
- Pre-notify the receiving obstetric or neonatal-capable service early
- If preterm birth is unavoidable, prepare actively for premature neonatal support and likely retrieval needs

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- A premature neonate is at especially high risk of hypothermia, so warmth should be planned before delivery if possible
- When in doubt about neonatal viability or resuscitation, begin supportive measures while clarifying the situation
- Record gestation if known, contraction pattern, membrane status, and whether delivery seemed imminent
